stimulus:
The following text is from James Weldon Johnson's 1917 poem “The Lantern.”

A lantern is so slight a thing,

And yet what cheer its wick can bring!

In days of work it warms my breast,

And when at dusk I crave for rest;

When carts come home along the lane,

And in the yard I hear the gate,

As Dusk, the keeper, tends his flame,

I light my lantern, and all is right.
stem: Which choice best states the main purpose of the text?

answerOptions.content: To portray the speaker's longing for the rural countryside
To convey how the simple act of lighting a lantern affects the speaker
To compare the speaker to a wandering lamplighter
To describe the chores the speaker performs at the end of each day

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. The text opens by declaring that a lantern is “so slight a thing” yet brings “cheer,” that it warms the speaker in days of work and in the evening when rest is craved. The closing lines repeat that by lighting the lantern the speaker feels “all is right.” The text as a whole is concerned with how this small act affects the speaker.

Choice A is incorrect because the rural details (carts, lanes, yards) serve as the setting in which the speaker lights the lantern, not as objects of longing. Choice C is incorrect because the “keeper” named in the poem is a figurative description of dusk, not a literal lamplighter the speaker is compared to. Choice D is incorrect because the text focuses on the speaker's feelings tied to the lantern, not on a list of end-of-day chores.
